critique.summary: The article reports that Honda is discontinuing its electric vehicle (EV) programs, including halting development of new models and stopping production of the Prologue, a GM-built EV. It argues that Honda's retreat from EVs risks leaving the company behind in the automotive industry's shift toward electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles.
mainPoints: Honda has stopped development of its electric Acura RDX, Honda 0 sedan and SUV, and will cease production of the GM-built Prologue.
The company blames U.S. tariffs and Chinese competition but lacked a strong EV strategy from the start.
Honda risks falling behind in the transition to electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles, which are becoming industry standards.
Legacy automakers like Honda often struggle with EV development due to reliance on internal combustion engine platforms.
Honda's identity as an internal combustion engine company and focus on driver-oriented cars may hinder its EV and software-defined vehicle efforts.
Honda's recent financial losses, especially in China, are partly attributed to its inability to compete with newer EV manufacturers on value and price.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are largely consistent with publicly available information. Honda has indeed announced halting development of certain EV models and stopping production of the Prologue, as reported by credible automotive news sources like Automotive News. The challenges faced by legacy automakers in transitioning to EVs and software-defined vehicles are well-documented industry trends. The article cites Honda's own earnings report acknowledging competitive struggles in China. However, the article is opinionated and speculative about Honda's future prospects and strategic motivations without direct statements from Honda executives beyond the cited earnings report. Overall, the factual claims about Honda's actions and industry context are accurate, but some conclusions about Honda's identity crisis and future risks are interpretive.
considerations: The article does not provide direct quotes or detailed statements from Honda leadership explaining the strategic decisions.
It does not explore Honda's broader global strategy, including potential EV plans in other markets or partnerships.
The impact of government policies, subsidies, and regulatory environments on Honda's EV strategy is mentioned but not deeply analyzed.
The article focuses mainly on negative implications without discussing any potential benefits or alternative strategies Honda might pursue.
The competitive landscape includes many legacy automakers facing similar challenges; Honda's situation is not unique.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Honda's decision to scale back its electric vehicle (EV) efforts, with commenters debating the viability of EVs, government and market influences, and Honda's strategic positioning. Opinions vary on the future of EVs, software-defined vehicles, and the broader automotive industry's transition.

comments.author: nytesky
comments.text:
Honda is an engine company at its heart. It makes very reliable, long lived engines.

They refine technology not really invent it (maybe invented VTEC). The transition to EV will be very gradual, I don’t even think we have enough rare earth metals and electrical grid capacity to go even twice as fast in adoption?

Honda is waiting for the standards and technology to settle out and become commodity technology, then they implement and iterate to a refined and reliable product.

It doesn’t seem like a winner take all market for EV? What would be the most? Perhaps I am ignorant on that part of market dynamics.

*edit for typos
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced understanding of Honda's core competencies and the challenges of EV adoption, reflecting realistic market and technological constraints.

comments.author: dangus
comments.text:
I don’t think the title is hyperbole. Toyota isn’t giving up on their long term EV R&D plans.

Just look at Nissan, which is broke as a joke, but they still put a new Leaf model on the market.

Lately there’s been a vibe that the EV experiment has died off, but that really isn’t true looking at industry reporting.

There is stalling that seems related to subsidy expiration and/or scale back, but we could argue that subsidies expiring is happening because the subsidies aren’t needed to sell vehicles anymore.

20% of new vehicles sold globally are EVs. Critical mass has been achieved, and not just in China (20% of vehicles sold in Europe are EVs).

This is also an admission that Honda is just giving up on Acura completely. That $50k two row luxury SUV buyer that is such an industry staple buyer for the US auto industry is going to be buying Rivian R2s instead of an EV Acura MDX.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately contextualizes Honda's move within the broader industry trends and highlights ongoing EV adoption despite some stalling.

comments.author: TrackerFF
comments.text:
I live in a top EV market, Norway.

ICE cars have been planned out for years now, and something like 96% of all new cars in Norway were EV last year.

Basically, if you plan on keeping selling ICE cars, you're removing yourself from the market here. There's no future for new personal ICE cars here.

I figure most other countries will be the same.
comments.analysis: Provides concrete data from a leading EV market, supporting the argument that ICE cars have limited future in such regions.

comments.author: jerlam
comments.text:
Do people really want "software defined vehicles"? People keep repeating how Tesla keeps upgrading their software, but I don't really want my car to change every time I step into it.

The person I know who loves FSD has soured on updates since the last one changed how the car handles simple things like intersections, and it's added a lot more stress.

Cars should be appliances, boring and reliable, not something to amaze and delight you. Especially since the latter usually changes into "sell ads and your personal information".
comments.analysis: Raises valid consumer concerns about software updates in vehicles, though this is somewhat subjective and depends on user preference.

comments.author: billfor
comments.text:
"Here, Honda is setting itself up for failure on the second disruption sweeping the automotive industry: the software-defined vehicle (SDV), which has core capabilities that can be upgraded and improved over time."

I'll pay triple for a non software defined vehicle that doesn't track me and can't be touched by the dealer once I purchase it. My one SDV (Tesla) is still on FSD from 2023 because the newer versions are terrible judging from the comments on the Tesla forums.
comments.analysis: Expresses a personal preference against software-defined vehicles, highlighting a counterpoint to the industry's direction but based on individual experience.

comments.author: bryanlarsen
comments.text:
OTOH, it really looks like Toyota is Goldilocks. Most companies invested too much too early and had to write off a substantial amount, but Toyota is rolling into 2027 with a small but nice selection of EV's.

Over 25% of vehicles sold world-wide were electric in 2025, and that percentage is steadily increasing. So VW & Ford were "too hot", Honda is looking like "too cold" and Toyota might be the "just right" of the three bears.
comments.analysis: Provides a balanced industry comparison, suggesting Toyota's cautious approach may be more sustainable than Honda's or others'.

comments.author: mullingitover
comments.text:
I'm convinced that the Japanese government is terrified of EVs because all the small and medium-sized businesses which support the Japanese auto industry will be absolutely gutted when vehicles contain drastically fewer parts.

That, and Japan is deeply screwed if they go all-in on EVs and then China decides they shouldn't be allowed access to any more rare earths.
comments.analysis: Offers a plausible geopolitical and economic explanation for Japan's cautious EV stance, though it is speculative.

comments.author: sys_64738
comments.text: Anything you need to plug into a power source is doomed to fail. EVs are simply not designed properly which is why hybrids are the best of both world. A Camry hybrid has some genius technology as the EV part is used at low speed and ICE at higher speed. That is the perfect balance and you see why it's a success for them. Toyota make the best hybrid vehicles. Honda makes hybrids too so they're not throwing all their EV technology into the e-waste bin.
comments.analysis: Advocates for hybrids over pure EVs citing design and practicality, reflecting a common perspective but somewhat generalized.

comments.author: gcanyon
comments.text:
EVs are going to be an extinction-level event for carmakers.

As the buggy-makers failed to transition to making cars, and thus ceased to be, so too will automakers fail to transition to EVs, and thus end their viability as vehicle manufacturers.
comments.analysis: Draws a historical analogy to emphasize the disruptive impact of EVs on traditional automakers, a valid but somewhat dramatic viewpoint.

comments.author: proee
comments.text: Could it be that the EVs they were planning were just out of touch with what the market wants? Their zero vehicles look butt-ugly in my opinion. They look like concept cars that are great for show, but no serious buyer would consider them for a daily driver.
comments.analysis: Critiques Honda's EV design from a consumer perspective, which is subjective but relevant to market acceptance.
